metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete grep-based proving command for AC1 that mechanically verifies both antipattern labels and both guard-set headers appear in the correct sections of the edited file.
findings.finding: AC1 has no mechanical proving command — the criterion that both antipatterns and guard sets appear in the correct sections relies on visual inspection, with no grep or diff anchor to produce evidence on success.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: `grep -c 'non-gating test' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-tests.md && grep -c 'unrealistic fault injection' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-tests.md && grep -c 'false-positive' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-tests.md` (adjust anchors to match actual section headings) — all must return ≥ 1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'supported CI environment' in the false-positive guard for the non-gating test antipattern, either as a pointer to a project-level canonical (e.g. a CI config file or env variable the reviewer can check with a tool call), or replace the oracle with a deterministic, self-contained litmus (e.g. 'if the skip condition tests for a platform/marker that no file in the diff's CI configuration declares as excluded, assume the condition holds in CI').
findings.finding: The non-gating test guard references 'the supported CI environment' as a decision criterion (e.g. 'a skip guarding a genuinely optional platform/dependency that the supported CI environment DOES exercise is NOT a finding'), but this phrase is undefined in the plan, in `code-review-tests.md`, and has no definition anywhere in the codebase. The executing LLM reviewer has no mechanism to resolve what CI environment the project uses, making the guard an ambiguous oracle that cannot be applied consistently.
findings.location: Plan section: 'non-gating test' antipattern, guard clause

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'stated removal condition' in the strict xfail guard with a concrete litmus — e.g., 'the `reason=` string (or an adjacent comment in the diff) references a ticket, issue number, or a named condition the executor can verify will eventually be false; a bare `reason=\'known failure\'` does NOT qualify'.
findings.finding: The strict xfail guard specifies 'a strict xfail (`strict=True`) tied to a stated removal condition is NOT a finding', but 'stated removal condition' is not defined — pytest's `xfail` has no formal 'removal condition' field, and the plan provides no litmus for what constitutes one (a comment? a linked ticket? a reason string matching a pattern?). An LLM executor implementing this guard has no parseable token to key on.
findings.location: Plan section: 'non-gating test' antipattern, guard clause for strict xfail

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one positive-detection eval case and one FP-guard (true-negative) eval/ledger entry for each new antipattern to `tests/unit/test_code_review_evals.py` or `test_code_review_fp_ledger.py`, and name them in the ACs so the new behaviors are verified end-to-end.
findings.finding: The plan adds two new antipattern detection behaviors to the reviewer prompt but the testing AC only requires existing tests to 'still pass' — no new eval cases or FP-ledger entries are called for to verify that the new antipatterns are actually detected by the reviewer and that the FP guards don't over-fire. This means the new behaviors could be added incorrectly (wrong phrasing, wrong guard) with no test catching it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'pytest tests/unit/test_code_review_evals.py tests/unit/test_code_review_fp_ledger.py' (4th AC bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC 4 to read: 'pytest … passes AND includes at least one new eval fixture for non-gating-test detection, one new FP-ledger entry for the strict-xfail guard, one new eval fixture for mis-seamed fault injection, and one new FP-ledger entry for the valid-monkeypatch guard.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one `expect: finding` (recall) and one `expect: pass` (no-fire) dataset entry to `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-tests.eval.yaml` for each of the two new antipatterns, or explicitly document why the existing cases provide sufficient coverage.
findings.finding: src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-tests.eval.yaml is a direct consumer of the code-review-tests prompt whose recall/no-fire dataset covers every antipattern the prompt's FLAG list names. The plan adds two new antipatterns (non-gating test, unrealistic fault injection/sleep-only sync) to the FLAG list but does not enumerate this eval spec as a file requiring new recall and no-fire cases for those antipatterns. AC4 only asserts existing tests 'still pass', leaving the new antipatterns without any eval-suite coverage.
findings.suggested_fix: Add two recall entries (e.g. R-nongating-skip with a diff containing a `pytest.mark.skip(condition=True)` visible in CI context, and R-fault-injection-wrong-seam with a diff monkeypatching the top-level command rather than the failing call) and two no-fire entries (e.g. FP-valid-strict-xfail and FP-sleep-as-secondary-mechanism) to the eval spec dataset.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale explaining why extending the existing `code-review-tests.md` overlay (rather than a separate overlay or eval-only seeding) is the correct approach — e.g., the overlay is the runtime-visible rubric the model reasons against, so new antipatterns must live there to be effective at inference time.
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for choosing the rubric-text-addition approach (vs. alternative vehicles such as a separate overlay or eval-only seeding), only negative constraints (no front-matter change, no routing change, no new file). Absence of a stated rationale is a G6 criterion gap.
findings.location: Plan body — 'What' section and Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence under 'What', e.g.: 'The overlay file is the runtime rubric the model consults during Pass-1; new antipatterns must be added there (not only to the eval dataset) to be effective at inference time, and the existing single-file structure avoids routing changes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one `expect: finding` recall seed to `code-review-tests.eval.yaml` for each new antipattern (e.g., `R-non-gating-skip` with a diff containing a `pytest.mark.skip` on a CI-run path, and `R-fault-injection-wrong-seam` with a diff replacing the top-level command rather than the failing call) so the eval suite can verify both new rules fire.
findings.finding: The plan requires the two new antipatterns to be added to the FLAG list and guard section of the rubric, but specifies no mechanism to verify that the new rules actually fire on real instances — no recall seeds are required in `code-review-tests.eval.yaml` for either new antipattern, even though every existing antipattern has one or more `expect: finding` recall seeds. AC4's pytest gate only validates the existing eval dataset, which won't catch a miscrafted new rule that the model ignores.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one recall (expect: finding) case and one no-fire (expect: pass) case to `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-tests.eval.yaml` for each of the two new antipatterns (non-gating test and unrealistic fault injection/sleep-only synchronization), and include a corresponding AC line requiring the eval spec to cover them.
findings.finding: The plan adds two new antipatterns with FP guards to `code-review-tests.md` but does not specify adding any recall (fire) or no-fire (pass) cases to `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-tests.eval.yaml` for the new antipatterns. The AC's test command (`pytest tests/unit/test_code_review_evals.py`) exercises `test_code_review_dataset_balanced`, which validates balance in the existing dataset, but the new antipatterns (non-gating test, unrealistic fault injection) have no seeded eval cases. A new decision rule with no eval coverage means the LLM could systematically fail to fire on these antipatterns with no AC gate catching it.
findings.location: AC line: '`make lint` passes and the code-review prompt/eval unit tests still pass (`pytest tests/unit/test_code_review_evals.py tests/unit/test_code_review_fp_ledger.py`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `code-review-tests.eval.yaml` dataset: a recall case with a diff showing `@pytest.mark.skipif(condition_that_holds_in_CI, ...)` on a new test (mode: non-gating-test), a no-fire case with a diff showing `@pytest.mark.xfail(strict=True, reason='...')` (mode: strict-xfail-valid), a recall case with a diff showing fault injected above the seam (mode: unrealistic-fault-injection), and a no-fire case showing fault injected at the real boundary with a deterministic synchronization mechanism.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the single canonical regenerate-index command (or the explicit fallback sequence) in AC2 so the executor need not choose.
findings.finding: AC2's proving command contains an unresolved fork ('or the legacy module path') that leaves the executor without a single authoritative command to run, making the verification step ambiguous.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical with the explicit fallback: `python -m rebar.llm.prompting.prompts regenerate-index 2>/dev/null || python -m rebar.llm.prompting.legacy_prompts regenerate-index` — and note which is primary.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
